# Welcome to Our Blog

## Industry insider insights from the professionals at UHC Solutions.

![How Economic Stability Shapes Health Outcomes | UHC Solutions](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20419'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![How Economic Stability Shapes Health Outcomes | UHC Solutions](https://www.uhcsolutions.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Client-Blog-Images-4-1024x536.png)

### How Economic Stability Shapes Health Outcomes

[Economic stability is one of the most significant social determinants of health](https://url.avanan.click/v2/___https:/www.cwla.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/SDOH-One-Pager-EconomicStability.pdf___.YXAzOnVoY3NvbHV0aW9uczphOm86M2UwNzQwMDM2NGYyNTIyYjc0MDcxODgyNTNmZjA5MTg6NjphNGE4Ojg1ZWJjZjVkNmIzY2Q1YWM2OTMwZmE0MGIxZjM2MTQxMDJiYjU3OTQwOWYzNWNiMjBmNTZmYTA1NGY1NjEwNmU6cDpGOk4), profoundly influencing individual and community well-being. Financial security affects access to essential resources like healthcare, housing, nutrition, and education, contributing to better health outcomes.

For many Americans, economic instability—whether caused by unemployment, underemployment, or living in poverty—creates barriers that result in poorer physical and mental health.

Healthcare providers, especially those working in community-based settings like Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs), are crucial in addressing these issues and promoting [health equity](https://url.avanan.click/v2/___https:/www.uhcsolutions.com/pursuing-health-equity-exemplary-best-practices-of-community-health-centers/___.YXAzOnVoY3NvbHV0aW9uczphOm86M2UwNzQwMDM2NGYyNTIyYjc0MDcxODgyNTNmZjA5MTg6NjozYzM3OmY1MzkyZDUyYTg4MjJlZjI3NjIxZjAxMzdlMjZjZjg1NjBiY2E3MmUxNWJjNDc5NDViMGExYTZmNjBjYTY1NGU6cDpGOk4).

#### How Can Economic Stability Shape Health Outcomes?

##### **The Link Between Economic Stability and Health**

[One-third of adults](https://url.avanan.click/v2/___https:/www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/feelings-of-financial-insecurity-in-america-soar-to-record-high-even-as-consumer-anxiety-about-the-economy-and-recession-recede-302083635.html___.YXAzOnVoY3NvbHV0aW9uczphOm86M2UwNzQwMDM2NGYyNTIyYjc0MDcxODgyNTNmZjA5MTg6NjoxZmNjOjdlYTBiNDI5ZWEwY2I0MGY4NGU5ZjA2MjliODZhN2Q1MzAzNzExZjcyMWNhOTRjZjFlYzRkM2M5Y2VhOTBiNGM6cDpGOk4#:~:text=One%2Dthird%20(33%25)%20of%20adults%20say%20they,do%20not%20feel%20financially%20secure.) say they are not economically secure. Economic stability is tied to a person’s ability to afford healthcare, healthy food, stable housing, and even a safe living environment. People with financial security are more likely to attend regular medical appointments, purchase necessary medications, and maintain a healthy lifestyle. Conversely, financial stress and instability often lead to delays in seeking care, neglecting preventive services, and difficulties in managing chronic health conditions.

A lack of economic stability can create a cycle of worsening health. For example, individuals experiencing job loss may lose health insurance, leading to delayed treatment of conditions that become more severe. Unemployment can also contribute to mental health issues such as anxiety and depression, which in turn can affect physical health. Economic instability limits access to these basic needs, disproportionately affecting vulnerable populations such as low-income families, minority communities, and individuals with disabilities.

##### **The Impact of Employment and Income on Health**

A stable income allows individuals to meet their basic needs, including paying for healthcare services, medications, and healthy food. Those with higher incomes tend to have better access to healthcare providers, enabling them to receive timely medical care and preventive services. Higher-income individuals are also more likely to afford healthy food options, leading to better nutrition and lower rates of obesity, diabetes, and heart disease.

Conversely, unemployment or low-income jobs often result in financial strain, contributing to stress-related health issues such as high blood pressure, sleep disorders, and mental health conditions. Many people in these situations may skip doctor visits, ration medications, or avoid seeking treatment altogether because of cost concerns. This situation further exacerbates health problems, resulting in higher rates of emergency room visits and hospitalizations for conditions that could have been managed or prevented with regular care.

##### **Barriers to Economic Stability**

Several factors contribute to economic instability, including educational attainment, job opportunities, and systemic inequities. Limited access to education and job training often traps individuals in low-paying jobs, preventing them from achieving financial security. In addition, racial and ethnic minorities face structural barriers that limit their access to high-paying jobs, fair housing, and quality healthcare.

Geographical location also plays a role. Rural areas often have fewer employment opportunities and healthcare resources, creating further barriers to economic stability and, consequently, health. Urban areas, while having more healthcare options, may face higher costs of living that strain individuals’ financial resources, even if employed.

##### **Addressing Economic Instability in Healthcare**

Healthcare providers, especially those in FQHCs, are in a unique position to help patients navigate the challenges of economic instability. FQHCs provide services on a sliding fee scale, ensuring that individuals with limited income can still access care. These centers often offer additional services such as mental health counseling, substance abuse treatment, and social work assistance, helping patients address the broader social determinants of health that affect their well-being.

By collaborating with community organizations, housing agencies, and local governments, FQHCs can connect patients to resources that help alleviate economic challenges. Programs that assist with job training, housing, and food security can contribute to improved health outcomes by addressing the root causes of financial instability.

##### **Healthy Body, Happy Life**

Economic stability is a crucial factor in shaping [health and wellness](https://url.avanan.click/v2/___https:/www.uhcsolutions.com/the-role-of-community-health-workers-in-promoting-health-and-wellness/___.YXAzOnVoY3NvbHV0aW9uczphOm86M2UwNzQwMDM2NGYyNTIyYjc0MDcxODgyNTNmZjA5MTg6Njo2YWU1OjhiMzgxNTNmZjcwOGY0NDYzMjQ4NGViNWY3YjUxYjEyYzY5ZjdlOTA0Y2Y0OWEyODU1NzRkMDNkYmMwZDQwZDk6cDpGOk4). Financial security allows individuals to access the care, nutrition, and resources they need to lead healthy lives. Conversely, economic instability creates barriers that prevent people from receiving adequate care and maintaining healthy lifestyles. Addressing economic instability requires a holistic approach, with healthcare providers, especially those in FQHCs, playing a vital role in connecting patients to essential resources. By understanding and addressing the financial challenges that impact health, healthcare professionals can help reduce disparities and improve the overall well-being of their communities.

#### Looking For Talent?

UHC Solutions supports these efforts by providing highly qualified staff to the FQHC community. We help these organizations find clinical and administrative talent, serving as a partner to help them meet their hiring goals. [Contact us](https://url.avanan.click/v2/___https:/www.uhcsolutions.com/contact-fqhc-recruitment/___.YXAzOnVoY3NvbHV0aW9uczphOm86M2UwNzQwMDM2NGYyNTIyYjc0MDcxODgyNTNmZjA5MTg6NjowY2FlOjgyMGEyN2FkNDM4ZDE1YzgyMjMzZmFkM2UwMGQzNzQ4YTVhZDk4MzU2MmFkZDcxZmRhNTI2N2QzNDZlYzUzMWQ6cDpGOk4) to find out what we can do for your organization.

Economic Stability
==================

![Image 1: HP2030 logo](https://odphp.health.gov/themes/custom/hp2030/images/hp2030-logo-lb.svg)

*   [Overview and Objectives](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/objectives-and-data/browse-objectives/economic-stability)
*   [Evidence-Based Resources](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/objectives-and-data/browse-objectives/economic-stability/evidence-based-resources)
*   [Healthy People in Action](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/objectives-and-data/browse-objectives/economic-stability/healthy-people-in-action)

Goal: Help people earn steady incomes that allow them to meet their health needs.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 2: A dollar sign icon overlays three houses on a neighborhood street.](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/styles/max_1300x1300/public/2020-07/EconomicStability_0.png?itok=y9MT44zt)

In the United States, 1 in 10 people live in poverty,[1](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/objectives-and-data/browse-objectives/economic-stability#cit1) and many people can’t afford things like healthy foods, health care, and housing. Healthy People 2030 focuses on helping more people achieve economic stability.

People with steady employment are less likely to live in poverty and more likely to be healthy, but many people have trouble finding and keeping a job. People with disabilities, injuries, or conditions like arthritis may be especially limited in their ability to work. In addition, many people with steady work still don’t earn enough to afford the things they need to stay healthy.

Employment programs, career counseling, and high-quality child care opportunities can help more people find and keep jobs. In addition, policies to help people pay for food, housing, health care, and education can reduce poverty and improve health and well-being.

### **Objective Status**

*   ![Image 3](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/2021-06/icon-status-targetMet-OL.svg)**1** Target met or exceeded
*   ![Image 4](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/2021-10/icon-status-improving-OL.svg)**4** Improving
*   ![Image 5](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/2021-06/icon-status-noChange-OL.svg)**2** Little or no detectable change
*   ![Image 6](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/2021-06/icon-status-worse-OL.svg)**2** Getting worse
*   ![Image 7](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/2021-06/icon_status_baseline-OL.svg)**0** Baseline only

# Economic Stability

[Social determinants of health (SDOH)](https://health.gov/healthypeople/priority-areas/social-determinants-health) are non-medical factors that influence health outcomes. They are the conditions in which people are born, live, learn, work, play, worship, and age, and the wider set of forces and systems shaping the conditions of daily life.

SDOH are grouped by [Healthy People 2030](https://health.gov/healthypeople) into five key areas. This discussion guide focuses on the area of [economic stability](https://health.gov/healthypeople/objectives-and-data/browse-objectives/economic-stability). This area refers to the connection between the financial resources people have—income, cost of living, and socioeconomic status—and their health.

If you have not already done so, please take the following steps:

![economic stability_file folders](/prepyourhealth/toolkits/00_images/economic_5_fb.jpg?_=38855)

### [Economic Stability](/prepyourhealth/discussionguides/CPR_PYH_335109-A_economic-stability_v11_508_FINAL.pdf)

Economic stability includes key issues, such as poverty, employment, food security, and housing stability. These issues can affect how people prepare for and respond to an emergency. For example,

The objectives of this discussion-based activity are to help emergency planners

Just promoting healthy choices and protective actions won’t eliminate the impacts of SDOH. Public health departments and their partners must act to understand and address conditions of economic stability in their planning and processes.

### Scenario Overview

The discussion guides in this series use the scenario of a mass evacuation. Mass evacuations are possible in response to many different types of emergencies, including hurricanes, chemical spills, and wildfires.

An evacuation order may raise questions in the minds of many people in the community.

Often, it’s not a matter of following an evacuation order; it’s also a matter of the financial means necessary to leave and stay healthy during an evacuation.

Please note that neither this scenario nor the discussion questions address all key issues associated with economic stability. Examples and evidence of SDOH impacts and interventions are likely to evolve with additional research and experience.

### Lessons from the Field

Review the below key issues and barriers that a mass evacuation might cause members of your community.

Think of past events that caused perceptible preparedness and response challenges in/to your community. Incorporate identified gaps and lessons learned from those events into your discussion. Lived experiences can add depth to your discussion.

### Key Issues & Barriers

Your community is under an evacuation order.

Many do not have transportation because they cannot afford a car or the gas required to run it. And they cannot afford a taxi or ride-share service.

Although they cannot afford to leave, they cannot afford to stay because their homes offer little in terms of “shelter from the storm.” If they do leave, they have no place to go and no means to take care of themselves. As a result, these community members will need evacuation and financial assistance.

Many people do not have an emergency fund.

### Discussion Questions

### After Your Discussion

A lot of useful information should come out of your discussion. Use this information to take actions that better prepare your community for an evacuation. Next steps may include the following:

### Promising Practice

Summer Faerman was on a walk in early 2020 when she saw a small wooden box at the entrance of a neighborhood. She investigated. The box was a free library, where people could take a book for themselves and leave them for others. The idea inspired her to create something similar—a self-serve food pantry.

Faerman started with one pantry in front of a The Salvation Army. It was a strategic choice. The Salvation Army is known in the community as a place to donate goods. It’s also a block away from a local elementary school and a public housing development. Over the past year, one pantry has turned into 52 [TLC Little Free Pantries](https://btcboca.org/littlefreepantry/) located around South Florida.

![NYC Logo](/IndicatorPublic/images/nyc-bubble-logo.svg)
![](/IndicatorPublic/7618350_heatmap_business_analytics_statistics_icon-gpr.ico)

[Environment & Health](/IndicatorPublic/)
Data Portal

# Economic stability creates health

![](/IndicatorPublic/data-stories/economic-stability/CrownHeights_JulienneSchaer-065_hu_b46b6c7ba1ece813.JPG)

Health is determined by many factors. People tend to be healthier when they have economic stability, live in quality housing and have access to public space and safe, connected communities. In 2020, the NYC Health Department held a series of community conversations with residents and local stakeholders across 26 neighborhoods. New Yorkers shared personal stories about their priorities for building healthier neighborhoods, healthier childhoods and more. This is one part of a three-part series on how  **housing, economic stability, and public space create health.**

**17 percent of New Yorkers, or about 1,400,000 people, live in poverty.** Living in poverty impacts health in many ways. These include:

Financial pressures: stress and anxiety from being unable to pay for all costs of living and having to choose whether to pay for rent, food, medical care or other necessities.

Unhealthy and unstable housing conditions: increased exposure to weather-related illnesses from not being able to afford air conditioning or heat. Lack of building maintenance can lead to housing conditions that result in pests, mold and dust that can worsen asthma and other medical conditions.

Food insecurity: lack of access to fresh food or enough food.

The combined effects of poverty are increased sickness and death.

### Poverty forces people to do without necessities that keep them healthy, like food and medical care.

Nearly one-third of renting households in the City experience severe rent burden â spending at least half their income on rent.

We see severe rent burden among New Yorkers of all income levels â but low-income people feel these effects more. Severe rent burden impacts nearly half (44%) of households that earn between $25,000 and $50,000, and 85% of households that earn less than $25,000. People who are rent burdened must make difficult choices between housing and necessities that support health. For example, they may choose not to use an air conditioner because of the electricity bill  [despite this increasing their risk for heat-related illness and death.](https://a816-dohbesp.nyc.gov/IndicatorPublic/beta/data-stories/heat/)

### One out of every 10 New Yorkers experiences food insecurity.

âI have to make sure that my children eat, I cannot see a starving child. I will go without food. If anything, I will go without a meal. But my children, I canât see them without food. I just canât.â

âParticipant in the [*Qualitative Study to Enhance the NYCgov Poverty Measure*](https://www.nyc.gov/assets/opportunity/pdf/evidence/nycgov-povertymeasure-enhancedstudy.pdf)

âParticipant in the [*Qualitative Study to Enhance the NYCgov Poverty Measure*](https://www.nyc.gov/assets/opportunity/pdf/evidence/nycgov-povertymeasure-enhancedstudy.pdf)

âParticipant in the [*Qualitative Study to Enhance the NYCgov Poverty Measure*](https://www.nyc.gov/assets/opportunity/pdf/evidence/nycgov-povertymeasure-enhancedstudy.pdf)

Low-income New Yorkers also have to make tradeoffs when it comes to food. Food insecurity (not always having enough food) became even worse during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Our health is shaped not just by having  *enough*  food to eat but also by having the  *right* foods to eat. NYC is known for its bodegas (mini-markets) but they donât always offer healthy staples. For foods that make up a healthy diet, New Yorkers need grocery stores. But most high-poverty neighborhoods in NYC have more bodegas than grocery stores â making healthier food harder to access than unhealthy food.

### With no money to spare after rent and food, many New Yorkers go without the health care or medicines they need.

Residents living in high-poverty neighborhoods are more likely to postpone health care. By going without these services, diseases can be more difficult to prevent, catch early, and treat, leading to more serious and costly health conditions.

Getting essential health care can mean the difference between staying afloat and winding up in medical debt.

Getting care for a medical issue can be costly, even with insurance. Medical debt occurs when people canât pay bills.

### Medical debt creates stress and disproportionately affects people of color.

Six percent of all New Yorkers â about 1 in 20 people â have medical debt in collections. People living in communities of color tend to experience a higher rate of, and owe more money in, medical debt than people living in White communities. In Brooklyn, for example, [communities of color have double the amount of residents with medical debt in collections.](https://apps.urban.org/features/debt-interactive-map/?type=medical&variable=medcoll&state=36&county=36047)  [A medical debt relief program is planned to help alleviate some of this burden.](https://www.nyc.gov/office-of-the-mayor/news/062-24/mayor-adams-relieve-2-billion-medical-debt-hundreds-thousands-working-class#/0)

Chronic stress related to finances can be debilitating because the factors causing it are difficult to resolve, and many worsen over time. Long-term stress increases the risk of mental health issues and other health problems.

### Racism shapes who experiences poverty â and its health effects â in NYC.

In NYC, as in most places in the United States, there are more Black and Latino residents living in poverty than residents of other races. [This is a product of racism](https://a816-dohbesp.nyc.gov/IndicatorPublic/beta/data-stories/housing/) and a history of policies that deprive people of color of access to home ownership, education and jobs, and other opportunities to build wealth and economic stability.

### New Yorkers who participated in community conversations describe ways to decrease poverty and improve health

Public health is about searching for and addressing the root causes of different health outcomes. The data are clear, and so are the personal stories that were shared: increasing economic stability would greatly improve health for New Yorkers.

To hear more from New Yorkers about how they view economic stability and health, listen to recordings from the community conversations:

enriched_points.expanded_content: Economic stability is crucial for health, influencing access to essential resources like healthcare, housing, and nutrition, which directly affect overall well-being and health outcomes.

Education enhances health literacy and employment opportunities, enabling individuals to make informed health decisions and access better economic conditions, which contribute to healthier lifestyles.
Access to healthcare is vital for preventive services, timely treatments, and management of chronic conditions, significantly impacting health outcomes and reducing disparities among communities.
The neighborhood environment influences health through factors like safety, access to recreational spaces, and availability of healthy food options, affecting physical and mental well-being.
Social context, including family, community networks, and cultural norms, shapes health behaviors and access to resources, impacting individual and community health outcomes.
